briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council Agenda Includes Territorial Acknowledgement Ceremony
briefs.summary: The Burnaby City Council meeting agenda for April 28, 2026 includes a Territorial Acknowledgement — a formal recognition of the Indigenous peoples on whose traditional lands the city sits. This is a standard ceremonial agenda item and does not propose any new rules, bylaws, or requirements for businesses.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Burnaby Open Meeting Agenda — April 28, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to meet on April 28, 2026, to consider items on the open council meeting agenda. The specific proposals and bylaw changes up for discussion have not been detailed in the available agenda text, so business owners should review the full agenda directly to identify any items that may affect their operations.
briefs.action_required: Check the full Burnaby Council agenda for April 28, 2026 to spot any items affecting your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby City Council open meeting — agenda items considered

briefs.title: Council to Consider: North Road BIA Renewal Abandoned After Petition Threshold Met
briefs.summary: At the April 14, 2026 Burnaby Council meeting, several items were considered — most notably, the proposed renewal of the North Road Business Improvement Area (BIA) bylaw was abandoned after enough business owners signed petitions against it. Council also referred a Canadian Federation of Independent Business letter opposing a Provincial Sales Tax expansion on professional services to staff for further review.
briefs.action_required: If you operate on North Road, watch for updates on the BIA renewal — the bylaw has been abandoned and your levy obligations may change.

key_dates.label: National Day of Mourning workplace safety event — New Westminster

briefs.title: Council to Consider Administrative Reports at April 28, 2026 Burnaby Meeting
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review and vote on a series of administrative reports at its April 28, 2026 meeting. The specific proposals within these reports have not been detailed in the agenda item provided, but decisions made could affect local businesses, development, or city operations in Burnaby.
briefs.action_required: Check the full April 28, 2026 Burnaby Council agenda for report details that may affect your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council administrative reports meeting date

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Replace 60-Year-Old Zoning Bylaw with New Rules Effective July 1, 2026
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on replacing Burnaby's Zoning Bylaw 1965 — the rulebook that controls what can be built and where — with a new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026. If approved, the new bylaw would take effect July 1, 2026, introducing height-based building rules, new design guidelines, stronger tenant protections, updated parking requirements, and new zoning categories for properties across the city.
briefs.action_required: Check if your property's zoning district is changing on July 1, 2026 — it may affect what you can build or operate.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 takes effect — new rules apply citywide
Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 — Preliminary Plan Approval transition deadline
Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 in-progress rezoning bylaws — adoption deadline

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Council to vote on $2M funding transfer to Burnaby Housing Authority for Year 2 operations
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving a $2.0 million operating funding transfer to the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation (BHA) — a city-owned body created to build and manage affordable and market rental housing. If approved, the money would come from the City's Operating Housing Reserve and cover the BHA's day-to-day costs for its second year of operation.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision — this shapes Burnaby's affordable housing supply pipeline.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — BHA Year 2 operating funding approval

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development, Finance & Insurance

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on 2026 property tax rates for all property classes
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on the 2026 property tax rates for Burnaby. A proposed bylaw would set the rate for businesses (Class 6) at $6.39 per $1,000 of assessed value — a combined 2.9% general increase plus a 1.9% growth infrastructure levy. Businesses in the Hastings BIA would also pay an additional $0.65 per $1,000 of assessed value.
briefs.action_required: Check your property class and review the proposed rates — your 2026 tax bill will reflect these if approved.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote on 2026 property tax rates bylaw
Burnaby 2026 property tax rates bylaw must be passed — provincial deadline
Hastings BIA — first tax instalment (30%) remitted to BIA
Hastings BIA — second tax instalment (70%) remitted to BIA
Burnaby MVRD regional tax remittance deadline

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Council to vote on appointing a new Approving Officer for Burnaby subdivisions
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on appointing Mark Norton, Manager of Development, as an Approving Officer — the city official authorized to approve subdivision applications (requests to divide or re-divide land). This would add Norton to a team of three existing Approving Officers, helping ensure applications are processed without delays when staff are unavailable.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision if you have a subdivision application pending in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Approving Officer appointment — council vote date

briefs.title: Council to vote on approving oversized retaining walls at 8012 11th Avenue, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a Development Variance Permit (DVP) — a special approval that allows a property to bend a specific zoning rule — for 8012 11th Avenue. The permit would allow retaining walls on the east and west sides of the property to stand taller than the 1.2 m limit set by city rules. The eastern wall reaches 2.43 m and the western wall reaches 2.06 m — both already built, and both partially crossing onto neighbouring properties.
briefs.action_required: If you own or occupy a nearby property, attend the April 28, 2026 Council meeting to share your feedback.

key_dates.label: Burnaby DVP #26-01 retaining wall variance — Council vote date

briefs.title: Council to vote on $4 million insurance contract renewals for Burnaby city assets
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving roughly $4 million in insurance premiums for 12 city insurance contracts, covering everything from buildings and equipment to cyber security and fine arts. The total cost is about $180,000 less than last year — a 4% drop — with the biggest savings coming from a 15% decrease on the main property insurance policy.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28 council decision — no direct action required for most business owners.

key_dates.label: Burnaby 2026 insurance programs renewal takes effect

briefs.title: Council to Consider: $4,000 for Two Heritage Commissioners to Attend Squamish Conference
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on spending up to $4,000 to send two members of Burnaby's Community Heritage Commission to the 2026 Heritage BC Conference in Squamish, BC, running May 4–6, 2026. The conference focuses on cultural heritage, industrial history, and community identity, and attending commissioners would report back their key learnings to the Commission afterward.

key_dates.label: 2026 Heritage BC Conference takes place in Squamish, BC

briefs.title: Council to vote on $1M+ networking software contract for Burnaby's IT systems
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract worth roughly $1,047,545 (including taxes) to Insight Canada Inc. for Cisco DNA software licenses — the digital tools that keep the City's internet, Wi-Fi, payment systems, and file-sharing running securely. The proposed three-year contract includes an option to renew for up to two additional one-year terms.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision — this affects City IT infrastructure spending.

key_dates.label: Burnaby networking software contract award — council vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on $1.96M furniture contract for five new Burnaby community facilities
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on awarding a contract worth roughly $1.96 million (plus a $196,260 contingency buffer) to Heritage Office Furnishings Ltd. to supply, deliver, and install furniture across five new city facilities opening over the next five years — including the Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre, Cameron Community Centre, and Brentwood Community Centre.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if your business supplies furniture or fit-out services.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — New Civic Facility furniture contract award

briefs.title: Council to vote on $47,000 contract increase for Burnaby's external audit services
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on adding $47,000 (including taxes) to its existing contract with KPMG LLP for external audit services. The extra cost covers additional audit work triggered by new public sector accounting rules — standards that govern how governments report their finances — that were not part of the original 2021 contract.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if you provide audit or accounting services to the City.

key_dates.label: Burnaby external audit services contract expiry — KPMG LLP

briefs.title: Council to vote on $384,893 contract increase for Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex ground testing
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on expanding the contract with WSP Canada Inc. for geotechnical consulting — that means expert soil and ground testing — at the Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex. The proposed contract change would add $384,893 (including taxes), plus a $73,500 contingency (a backup fund for unexpected costs), bringing the total contract value to roughly $1.1 million.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if you work in construction or supply services to this project.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex geotechnical contract change

briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review and vote on committee reports at its April 28, 2026 meeting. key_dates.label: Burnaby City Council committee reports meeting date

briefs.title: Council to vote on $215,000+ in Spring 2026 community grants for Burnaby nonprofits
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving 31 community grants totalling over $215,000 for the Spring 2026 intake. The grants would support local sports teams, food banks, newcomer services, cultural events, and social programs — spread across five grant categories including Recreation Sports, Operating, Initiative, Subsidized Use of City Facilities, and Food Security.
briefs.action_required: If your nonprofit applied, check the eligibility list — 20 applications were rejected for failing to meet grant policy requirements.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Spring 2026 Community Grant applications — Council vote

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to raise recreation fees and launch Hotel Burnaby pricing
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on mid-year changes to Burnaby's fee bylaw — a bylaw that sets what residents and businesses pay for city services. The proposed changes would update Parks, Recreation and Culture fees starting June 1 and September 1, 2026, add room rates for the new Hotel Burnaby, and lower the age for a free Seniors Lifetime recreation membership from 90 to 80.
briefs.action_required: Check the updated Burnaby fee schedule if your business uses city recreation or hospitality facilities.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw — Schedule F1 (Hospitality/Hotel Burnaby) takes effect
Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw — Schedule F3 (Recreation & Culture fees) takes effect

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 — Council to vote on new renter rules
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed Tenant Protection Bylaw (TPB) that would replace the current Tenant Assistance Policy and extend renter protections to redevelopments that don't require rezoning — a gap in the existing rules. If approved, the bylaw would take effect July 1, 2026, and would require developers to give written notice, pay financial compensation, cover moving costs, and offer displaced tenants the right to return to a replacement unit at their old rent.
briefs.action_required: If you own or develop rental property in Burnaby, review the proposed bylaw now — it takes effect July 1, 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 proposed effective date — takes effect
Burnaby Council vote on Tenant Protection Bylaw — public meeting

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Council to vote on Southeast Bikeway Upgrade: new bike lanes, signals, and traffic changes
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a package of cycling improvements called the Southeast Bikeway Upgrade Project. If approved, the plan would add protected bike lanes, upgrade traffic signals, create multi-use pathways near schools, and change traffic flow on 12th Avenue — affecting roads connecting Edmonds Town Centre, Big Bend, and nearby neighbourhoods.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near 12th Avenue, Armstrong Avenue, or North Fraser Way, review the proposed road changes now.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Southeast Bikeway Upgrade — Council approval vote

briefs.title: Council to Consider Adding Protected Intersection Design to Burnaby Town Centre Street Standards
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on updating the Town Centre Design Standards to include 'protected intersection geometry' — a street design that adds physical barriers, set-back crossings, and refuge islands to keep cyclists and pedestrians safer where roads meet. If endorsed, the new design rules would apply to newly built intersections on major streets in Brentwood, Metrotown, Lougheed, and Edmonds as those areas redevelop.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if your business is near a Burnaby Town Centre redevelopment area.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards amendment — Council vote date

briefs.title: Council to Consider Burnaby Consent Agenda Items — April 28, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a package of items grouped under the Consent Agenda at its April 28, 2026 meeting. A Consent Agenda bundles routine or non-controversial items into a single vote — any item can be pulled out for separate discussion if a councillor requests it. The specific items have not been detailed in the source material provided.
briefs.action_required: Check the full April 28, 2026 Burnaby Council agenda for items that may affect your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council Consent Agenda vote — April 28, 2026 meeting

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Brentwood Park Cannot Be Exempted from Provincial Housing Rules
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a staff report explaining why Brentwood Park cannot be exempted from BC's small-scale multi-unit housing (SSMUH) rules — the provincial law that requires cities to allow triplexes, fourplexes, and small apartment buildings on most residential lots. Staff found no legal way to grant the exemption and do not recommend changing the Building Bylaw city-wide to enforce old private land restrictions (called Restrictive Covenants), warning it would slow down permits and raise costs for owners across Burnaby.
briefs.action_required: If you own property in Brentwood Park, watch for the April 28 council decision — it may affect your development options.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — Brentwood Park SSMUH exemption report considered

briefs.title: Council to receive Burnaby building permit activity report for March 2026
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a routine information report covering all building permits issued in Burnaby between March 1 and March 31, 2026. No decisions or new rules are being voted on — this report simply keeps Council informed about construction activity happening across the city.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Heritage Documentation for Forest Lawn Memorial Park Chapel Before Demolition
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to receive — for information only — two heritage documents about Forest Lawn Memorial Park at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue: an as-found report (a detailed record of the Chapel building before it is torn down) and a Statement of Significance (a document explaining what makes the site historically important). These were required as conditions of a rezoning application that, if finally approved, would allow the Chapel to be demolished and replaced with a new two-storey funeral home facility.
briefs.action_required: If you have ties to Forest Lawn Memorial Park, review the heritage documents before the April 28, 2026 Council meeting.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — heritage documents for Forest Lawn Chapel received for information

briefs.title: Council to Consider Burnaby's 2025 Solid Waste and Recycling Annual Report
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive the 2025 Solid Waste and Recycling Annual Report for information at its April 28, 2026 meeting. The report shows the city collected 74,489 tonnes of waste in 2025, hit a 61% diversion rate, and outlines upcoming plans like flexible plastics pilots, more cleanup events, and expanded recycling outreach.
briefs.action_required: If your business generates construction waste, review the C&D Waste Diversion Bylaw — a higher diversion threshold may be coming.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's Update on Plant-Based Food Options at City Facilities
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive an update on food sustainability efforts at city-run cafes and restaurants. The report — up for consideration at the April 28, 2026 meeting — outlines steps already taken to add plant-based, vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free menu items at places like Riverway and Mintara, and explains how staff plan to keep expanding these options based on customer demand and financial performance.
briefs.action_required: Watch the April 28 council meeting if your business supplies food or beverages to Burnaby city facilities.
briefs.industries: Food Service, Grocery & Food Retail, Agriculture

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to Consider Pedestrian Safety Upgrades Near Metrotown Parkade
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on receiving a report about planned safety improvements at the busy intersection of Central Boulevard and the Metropolis at Metrotown underground parkade entrance. The proposed changes include a 'pedestrian scramble' — a signal phase where all cars stop and people can cross in any direction, including diagonally — plus upgraded curb ramps, better accessible signals, and removal of a right-turn island to reduce pedestrian exposure to traffic.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near Metrotown, watch for construction updates starting Spring 2026 that may affect access.

key_dates.label: Central Boulevard parkade intersection construction completion target

briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review information reports at its April 28, 2026 meeting. No body text or details were provided for this agenda item, so the specific topics under consideration are not yet known.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on adding hotel, restaurant uses and 362 units at Lougheed towers
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend an already-approved rezoning for two high-rise towers at 3900 Grand Promenade and 6950 Austin Road. The changes would swap some planned office space for hotel and restaurant uses, add 362 more residential units, make both towers taller, and shift an affordable housing requirement to a nearby site called Carrigan.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this could affect your plans if you operate or lease space near the Lougheed development.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 7, 2026 — council consideration date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Food Service

briefs.title: Proposed: 12-Storey Mixed-Use Tower at 6535 Kingsway Up for Burnaby Council Approval
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed zoning bylaw (Bylaw No. 14809) that would rezone 6535 Kingsway to allow a 12-storey building with 290 homes and ground-floor commercial space facing Kingsway, plus underground parking. If approved, the rezoning — a change to the rules about how land can be used — would allow developer Intracorp to move forward with the mixed-use project.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council vote — approval could open new commercial leasing opportunities at 6535 Kingsway.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on rezoning 2757 Norland Avenue for self-storage facility
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone (change the permitted land use for) a property at 2757 Norland Avenue in Burnaby. If approved, the rezoning would allow a developer to build a five-storey enclosed self-storage facility on the site.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this rezoning could affect nearby property use and development plans.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on new bylaw — first, second, and third reading
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on giving a proposed bylaw its first, second, and third readings at the April 28, 2026 meeting. 'Readings' are the formal steps a bylaw must pass through before it becomes law — three readings at one meeting means Council could move quickly to adopt it. The full details of the bylaw's content were not included in the agenda item provided.
briefs.action_required: Check the full Burnaby Council agenda for April 28, 2026 to see what this bylaw covers.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — proposed bylaw first, second, and third reading

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Set New Rules for E-Bikes and Electric Kick Scooters
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update Burnaby's Street and Traffic rules to cover e-bikes (electric-assist bicycles) and electric kick scooters. The bylaw would set where these devices can ride and park, ban riding on sidewalks unless posted signs allow it, and restrict electric kick scooters from major roads unless a protected bike lane or multi-use pathway is available.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if you operate a shared micromobility service or delivery fleet, this could affect your operations.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Parks Bylaw to Allow E-Bikes and Electric Kick Scooters in Designated Areas
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update Burnaby's Parks Regulation Bylaw to officially include e-bikes (motor assisted cycles) and electric kick scooters under park rules. The change would make it clear that these devices can only be ridden or parked in parks where they are specifically permitted — not everywhere.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if you operate e-bike or scooter rentals in Burnaby parks, this affects where you can run.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw to Add E-Bikes and Electric Scooters to Park Rules
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would update Burnaby's park rules to explicitly include e-bikes (electric-assist bicycles) and electric kick scooters. The proposed change would make it a ticketable offence — with a $200 fine ($160 if paid early) — to ride or park these devices in park areas not designated for their use.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if you operate e-bikes or e-scooters in Burnaby parks, this affects you.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 Property Tax Rates Up for Council Approval
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on Bylaw No. 14814, which would set the property tax rates for all land and buildings in Burnaby for 2026. A proposed bylaw would also apply a special local tax (an extra levy charged to businesses in a defined area) to properties in the Hastings Business Improvement Area.
briefs.action_required: Check your property class and review the proposed mill rates — they determine your 2026 tax bill.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 — council vote at meeting
Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 — adoption deadline under Community Charter

briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on scheduling a Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee (PRCC) meeting for Wednesday, May 27, 2026, at 5:00 pm in the Council Chamber. This is a procedural step — it simply sets the date and time for the committee to meet, with no business decisions made yet.
